title: FalsifyBench: Evaluating Inductive Reasoning in LLMs with Rule Discovery Games
authors: Leonardo Bertolazzi (University of Trento), Katya Tentori (University of Trento), Raffaella Bernardi (Free University of Bozen-Bolzano)
venue: Not stated in the paper (preprint only; no conference/journal venue listed). arXiv:2606.04751v1 [cs.AI], submitted 03 Jun 2026.
claim: The paper introduces FalsifyBench, a benchmark generalizing the Wason 2-4-6 hidden-rule discovery task to semantic taxonomies (WordNet), in which an LLM 'player' must discover a hidden general category by iteratively proposing test triples and a hypothesis, receiving Conform/Do-not-conform feedback from an LLM 'oracle', until it guesses the rule. Across 12 LLMs, reasoning models are generally stronger scientific reasoners than instruction-tuned models, but no model approaches optimal performance, and the primary driver of success is the capacity for negative testing (falsification-seeking) rather than confirmation-seeking behavior, with turn-level analysis further tying failure to identifiable hypothesis-navigation patterns (drift into partial-overlap/disjoint hypotheses and surface-level linguistic hypotheses).
novelty: Authors present three contributions (Sec 1): (1) FalsifyBench itself -- a benchmark grounded in semantic taxonomies (not numeric sequences, to avoid 2-4-6 pretraining contamination) that operationalizes falsification/hypothesis-revision as a component of scientific reasoning; (2) an evaluation of 12 LLMs across families/scales showing reasoning models are stronger 'scientific thinkers' though none is close to optimal; (3) a fine-grained turn-level analysis (tracking the set relation between current hypothesis H and target rule R at every turn, via an offline LLM annotator) that they say prior related work (WILT, Jhaveri et al. 2026) neglects -- specifically, prior Wason-inspired LLM benchmarks neither sample from the H⊂R (too-narrow-hypothesis) configuration that makes negative testing uniquely optimal, nor track how per-turn hypotheses relate to the target rule (Sec 2, 'Neither study explicitly takes into account the normative framework introduced by Klayman and Ha (1987)...').

protocol.description: Fully interactive, multi-turn agentic protocol, not single-shot. One LLM instance plays the 'player' role (stateful across the game, up to 20 turns) and a separate API call of an LLM (same model family per game) plays the 'oracle' role (stateless -- each oracle call is freshly initialized with only the info needed for that judgment; Appendix C.4). The player alternates between two JSON-structured actions each turn: 'test' (propose 3 items + a hypothesis string + a reasoning trace) or 'guess' (propose a single property string). The oracle replies with fixed strings ('Conform'/'Do not conform' for tests; 'Correct'/'Incorrect' for guesses). A game ends when the player guesses correctly or the 20-turn cap is reached (Sec 3.1).
protocol.observation_content: Per Table 5 (verbatim system + first user prompt): the player's SYSTEM PROMPT states the game rules, the 20-turn budget, the exact oracle reply strings to expect, the two allowed action types, and the required JSON schema for each action type (test: items/hypothesis/reasoning; guess: property/reasoning). The player's FIRST USER MESSAGE gives only the 3 initial example items (drawn from a taxonomic descendant category S of the hidden target R) and asks 'What is your first move?'. The player is NEVER shown the target rule R or the sampling category S directly (Sec 3.2/App C.2: 'The player was never shown the target rule R or the sampling category S directly; it observed only the three example items and the system prompt'). On each subsequent turn the player's conversation history accumulates ALL prior oracle feedback and its own prior actions (stateful; App C.4) -- i.e. the observation grows turn by turn rather than being a fixed one-shot prompt. The oracle, called separately per turn with three distinct prompt templates (Table 6): (a) judge a test triple against the hidden target_property, returning JSON {conforms, explanation}; (b) classify the player's test-strategy intention (confirm vs falsify) plus judge conformance, returning JSON {intention, expected_conforms, conforms, explanation}; (c) judge a guess for semantic equivalence to target_property, returning JSON {correct, explanation}. A separate offline annotator model (GPT-5-Mini) is given a closed-label prompt (Table 7) to classify the set relation between H and R at each Test turn (identical/disjoint/partial_overlap/hypothesis_included_in_target/target_included_in_hypothesis), used only for post-hoc analysis, not gameplay.

representations: The paper does not vary input representation/format as an experimental axis (no ablation over prompt phrasing, item encoding, or output format) -- prompts were used verbatim and fixed across all models (Appendix C.1: 'The prompts we used verbatim in all experiments are shown in Tables 5-7'). The only structural manipulation is the WordNet taxonomic sampling design itself: items are always sampled from a category S that is a strict taxonomic descendant of the target rule R, deliberately engineering the H⊂R (hypothesis-too-narrow) configuration so that negative testing is the theoretically optimal strategy (Sec 3.1). Reasoning effort is varied per-model as a run-time parameter, not a representation: reasoning models were run at 'medium' effort as player, 'low or no reasoning' as oracle (depending on model), and 'low reasoning' during malformed-JSON retries to speed regeneration (Appendix D.1) -- this is a compute/strategy variant, not an input-format variant, and no accuracy breakdown by reasoning-effort level is reported.

models.name: GPT-5.2-Chat (instruction-tuned label per paper's 'reasoning' grouping is actually reasoning-side; used via Azure OpenAI API)
strategies: multi-turn agentic play/oracle roles, JSON-structured actions, medium reasoning effort as player
models.key_results: Best overall: 75% success rate (Sec 4, 'Player Performance'); Confirmation bias 27.3% (Sec 4); highest turn efficiency, 5.61 turns avg to solve (Appendix E.1/Fig 8); conclusive falsification rate 49.5% (Sec 5/Fig 5); oracle Cohen's kappa 0.98 on both Test and Guess turns, the best of all models (Sec 4/Fig 2); ~100% success rate on 'animal' target category (Appendix E.3); in failed games showed the largest gap (+27.0pp) toward partial-overlap turns vs successful games (Sec 6/Fig 6).

models.name: GPT-OSS-120B
models.key_results: 68% success rate (Sec 4); conclusive falsification rate 53.4%, second-highest (Sec 5); +22.3pp partial-overlap gap failed vs successful games (Sec 6).

models.name: GPT-OSS-20B
models.key_results: Confirmation bias 24.2%, lowest reported explicitly alongside GPT-5-Mini/GPT-5.2-Chat cluster (Sec 4); highest conclusive falsification rate of all models at 59.2% (Sec 5/Fig 5).

models.name: GPT-5-Mini
strategies: reasoning model, used as offline turn-level relation annotator (Sec 5)

models.name: GPT-5-Nano
models.key_results: 12% success rate, worst of all 12 models, worse than all instruction-tuned models (Sec 4); conclusive falsification rate 26.6%, second-lowest (Sec 5); no significant success/failure gap in surface-level linguistic hypothesis use (36.4% vs 25.0%; Sec 6).

models.name: Qwen3.5-9B
models.key_results: 21% success rate, second-worst (Sec 4); no significant surface-linguistic-hypothesis gap between successful/failed games (62.0% vs 47.6%; Sec 6).

models.name: GLM-5
strategies: reasoning model (Sec 4/6 text groups GLM-5 with GPT-5.2-Chat and GPT-OSS-120B as 'reasoning models'; its 58% success rate is incompatible with the paper's stated instruction-tuned cluster of 33-35%)
models.key_results: 58% success rate; oracle kappa 0.78/0.76 (Sec 4); +21.4pp partial-overlap gap failed vs successful, explicitly grouped among 'reasoning models' showing this pattern (Sec 6); lowest average guesses per game, 0.97 (Appendix E.1); 97.6% of GLM-5's failed games contained at least one surface-level linguistic hypothesis (Sec 6).

models.name: Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct-FP8
models.key_results: Lowest conclusive falsification rate, 18.0% (Sec 5); one of the highest Confirmation bias scores, 81.8% (Appendix E.1); highest average guesses per game, 6.92 overall (Appendix E.1) and 'nearly 8 guesses across all target properties' i.e. consistently high regardless of semantic domain, not specific to any one category (Appendix E.3); 8.5 in failed games vs 3.8 in successful games (Appendix E.4) -- described as relying on 'repeated guessing rather than active falsification'.

models.name: Mistral-Small-24B-Instruct-2501
models.key_results: Lowest oracle Cohen's kappa reported, 0.34 on Guess turns (Sec 4).

models.name: Kimi-K2.5
models.key_results: Low average guesses per game, 0.89, alongside GLM-5 (Appendix E.1).

headline_numbers: Best instruction-tuned model DeepSeek-V3.1: 41% success rate vs top reasoning models GPT-5.2-Chat 75% and GPT-OSS-120B 68% (Sec 4, Fig 2 top-left).
Small reasoning models underperform: GPT-5-Nano 12%, Qwen3.5-9B 21% -- worse than all instruction-tuned models (Sec 4).
Confirmation bias vs success rate: Spearman rho = -0.779, p = 0.003 (Sec 4).
Confirmation bias vs conclusive falsification rate: Spearman rho = -0.937, p < 0.001 (Sec 5, Fig 5 bottom).

Bayesian mixed-effects logistic regression (n=1,200 games): Confirmation bias coefficient -4.148 (SD 0.116), OR 0.016, 95% CI [-4.375,-3.921] (robustly negative); oracle error coefficient -0.123 (SD 0.147), OR 0.885, 95% CI [-0.411,0.165] (crosses zero, not credible) (Appendix D.2).
Oracle raw accuracy vs human annotation: mostly 85-95%; Cohen's kappa ranges 0.34 (Mistral-Small-24B, Guess turns) to 0.98 (GPT-5.2-Chat, both conditions) (Sec 4).
1,200 Test-turn and 1,200 Guess-turn instances human-annotated (100 games x 12 models) (Sec 3.2, footnote 4).
Curated benchmark = 100 games spanning 5 WordNet categories (animal, artifact, body part, food, plant), drawn from a larger pool of 17,905 possible games across 7 candidate categories (Sec 3.1).
Target-rule decomposition: 'artifact' is uniformly hardest (0-30% success for most models); 'animal' easiest, GPT-5.2-Chat reaching 100% success (Appendix E.3).
Total experiment compute cost approx. $150 (Appendix D.1).
failure_modes: (1) Confirmation bias / positive-testing dominance: instruction-tuned models cluster at 66-82% Confirmation bias vs reasoning models at 24-39% (Sec 4); positive tests are largely uninformative in the engineered H⊂R configuration, so high positive-test rates directly suppress conclusive falsifications (Sec 5). (2) Failed games show a significantly higher proportion of partial-overlap and disjoint Test-turn relations between H and R than successful games (Mann-Whitney U, p<0.05 for most models), i.e. failing players drift off the clean taxonomic upward-revision path (Sec 6, Fig 6 left; Appendix E.2, Fig 9). (3) Surface-level linguistic hypotheses: models sometimes fixate on orthographic/phonological features of item names (e.g. 'all items start with letter a', 'have exactly three vowels') rather than semantic category; this is systematically associated with failure for stronger models (GLM-5 97.6%, GPT-5.2-Chat 92.0%, GPT-OSS-120B 84.4% of their failed games contained such a hypothesis; Fisher's exact test p<0.05), whereas weak models (GPT-5-Nano, Qwen3.5-9B) show no significant difference, suggesting they fail for other/more basic reasons (Sec 6). (4) Passive over-guessing: Llama-4-Maverick submits far more guesses per game (avg 6.92 overall, 'nearly 8... across all target properties' i.e. uniformly high across categories rather than artifact-specific, 8.5 in failed games vs 3.8 in successful games) combined with high Confirmation bias (81.8%), indicating reliance on trial-and-error guessing rather than targeted falsification (Sec 4, Appendix E.1, E.3, E.4). (5) Target-rule-specific difficulty: the semantically heterogeneous 'artifact' category is uniformly hardest across all models and all metrics (lowest success, highest Confirmation bias, lowest conclusive falsification rate) (Appendix E.3). (6) Some strong models reach the correct hypothesis internally but fail to commit to a Guess before the turn limit (Appendix E.2). (7) Oracle quality is only weakly related to player failure -- oracle error rate has a near-zero, non-credible regression coefficient, and models with broadly comparable oracle kappa (MiniMax-M2.5: 0.67-0.91, GLM-5: 0.78/0.76, GPT-5-Nano: 0.81/0.72 -- three distinct, not identical, ranges) show widely divergent success rates (35%, 58%, 12%) (Sec 4).
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper is entirely about multi-step/interactive behavior; it does not report or compare a single-shot ('state the rule from the 3 examples directly') condition at all. Its central empirical claim is specifically about what happens ACROSS a multi-turn interaction loop: success requires accumulating negative (falsifying) evidence over many turns because the initial 3 examples alone are consistent with an overly narrow hypothesis (this mirrors Wason's 2-4-6 finding, Sec 1, footnote 3), and models must revise upward turn-by-turn. The paper's mixed-effects analysis (game-level, n=1200) and turn-level relation-tracking analysis are both premised on the existence of an extended trajectory of Test/Guess actions -- there is no ablation isolating how much of the gap comes from one demonstration vs many, or from more few-shot examples up front vs interactive querying. The paper's conclusion is that the differentiator between models is not raw pattern-matching from the initial demo but interactive strategy (negative vs positive testing) sustained across the turn budget (Sec 7 Conclusion): 'models that actively seek to falsify their hypotheses consistently outperform those that seek to confirm them, and our turn-level analysis showed that disconfirmatory behavior is a reliable proxy for progress toward the correct hypothesis.' Turn efficiency data (Appendix E.1, Fig 8) shows most models take 8-11 turns to succeed (when they do), with GPT-5.2-Chat notably efficient at 5.61 turns -- but no model is evaluated with a fixed, small number of turns/demos as a controlled variable, so no explicit 'few-shot count vs accuracy' curve is reported.

relation_to_candidate.position: FalsifyBench sits on the opposite end of the family's protocol axes from c23's design intent: it is fully interactive/multi-turn (up to 20 player-oracle rounds per game, stateful player history), the model observes a growing conversation state (3 initial items, then accumulating Test/Guess actions and oracle feedback) rather than a fixed few-shot demo block plus one query, the model must actively construct its OWN evidence-gathering queries (choosing which triples to test) rather than passively receiving demonstrations, the output per turn is a structured multi-field JSON action (test triple + hypothesis + reasoning, or a guess) rather than a single short transformed string or an MC letter, and the verifier is itself an LLM oracle (imperfect, measured against human annotation at 85-98% kappa) rather than a deterministic exact-match transducer. Rule/category complexity is not dialed via composed independent sub-rules but via WordNet taxonomic depth and category choice (5 domains, close vs deep sampling distance).
relation_to_candidate.overlap: Both papers descend from the same broader 'infer a hidden rule from evidence and apply/state it' lineage, and the corpus notes group them adjacent via the WILT / HERO'S JOURNEY cluster (per c23.md's 'Related active research' section and the corpus_claim). Both are concerned with whether models can induce an underlying rule rather than merely pattern-match surface features, and both authors' related-work sections independently cite Lake & Baroni (SCAN) and Chollet (ARC) as foundational induction-benchmark ancestors (FalsifyBench Sec 2), which are also explicit c23 literature parents. FalsifyBench's turn-level finding that models fixate on 'surface-level linguistic features' (e.g., letter/syllable/rhyme patterns) instead of true semantic rules is thematically close to c23's stated risk that a 'one-shot guessable' or overly transparent single rule can be solved by a shortcut sentence without real induction.
relation_to_candidate.differences: c23 is explicitly single-call (per candidate file: 'a smooth incremental ladder' scored by exact match against a reference transformer, no interaction loop; the dead-branches note explicitly excludes HERO'S JOURNEY/WILT/ARISE because 'their multi-step episodic form violates the single-call constraint'). FalsifyBench's entire design -- and its main empirical finding (interactive negative-testing strategy predicts success far more than raw model capability alone) -- depends on the multi-turn loop and is not applicable to c23's single-shot setting. FalsifyBench's ground truth/verifier is a same-model-family LLM oracle validated only against a partial (1,200-instance) human-annotated sample with kappa as low as 0.34 for some models, whereas c23's oracle is a fully deterministic, independently-sampled-and-computed transducer with exact match and no LLM-judge noise (a stronger measurement guarantee that c23's own 'Measurement skeptic' perspective already flags as a virtue). FalsifyBench's rules are single semantic categories with graded taxonomic distance (not composed independent sub-rules), so it has no analogue to c23's core 'K independent rule' incremental-ladder / distractor-in-exactly-one-rule structure. FalsifyBench's domain is natural-language semantic category membership (contamination risk mitigated only by avoiding the literal 2-4-6 numbers, not by inventing vocabulary), whereas c23 uses invented tokens/vocabularies specifically for contamination-proofing.
relation_to_candidate.what_c23_would_add: c23 would add: (1) a genuinely single-forward-pass evaluation of rule induction, isolating whether a model can extract K composed latent rules from static demonstrations without any adaptive querying -- a capability FalsifyBench's design cannot isolate because success there is confounded with interactive strategy (negative-testing behavior) and turn-budget management; (2) a deterministic, oracle-independent ground truth (no LLM-judge noise, unlike FalsifyBench's oracle kappa as low as 0.34); (3) a smooth, quantifiable partial-credit incremental ladder (how many of K rules stated/applied) versus FalsifyBench's binary per-game success/failure outcome; (4) contamination-proofing via invented vocabulary/tokens rather than reliance on a well-known lexical resource (WordNet) that is public and could itself be memorized; (5) much cheaper/faster instances (single call vs up to 20 sequential turns per game, at ~$150 total cost for only 1,200 FalsifyBench games), enabling many more instances per dollar for a quick-test harness.

corpus_claim: FalsifyBench (Bertolazzi et al., 2026) evaluates inductive reasoning with rule-discovery games; adjacent to c23 with a Wason / multi-turn flavor, overlapping the corpus's WILT / HERO'S JOURNEY cluster.
corpus_claim_verdict: supported. The paper verifiably (a) evaluates inductive reasoning via 'rule discovery games' explicitly inspired by the Wason 2-4-6 task (Abstract, Sec 1: 'an evaluation framework for hypothesis-driven reasoning inspired by the classic Wason 2-4-6 task'), (b) is unambiguously multi-turn/interactive (up to 20 turns, stateful player, Sec 3.1), and (c) the paper's own Related Work section (Sec 2) explicitly places itself alongside WILT (Banatt et al., 2024) as a closely related Wason-2-4-6-inspired multi-turn inductive logic benchmark, directly substantiating the 'overlapping the corpus's WILT ... cluster' portion of the claim. No claim in the dossier text needed correction; the FalsifyBench paper does not itself mention HERO'S JOURNEY, so that half of the clustering is a corpus-side grouping decision (found in c23.md) rather than something FalsifyBench asserts about itself -- this is a minor scope note, not a miscitation, since the corpus_claim is framed as the corpus's own clustering judgment, not a claim attributed to the paper.
baseline_relevant: Instance count/scale used: 100 curated games (5 target categories x mixed close/deep sampling distance) drawn from a larger generatable pool of 17,905 possible games across 7 WordNet target categories (Sec 3.1); useful ceiling reference for how many instances a taxonomy-based generator can cheaply produce vs how many are actually needed for stable comparisons.

Difficulty dial used: taxonomic distance from target category, split into 'close' (<=4 edges) and 'deep' (>4 edges) sampling categories, sampled in equal proportion per game (Sec 3.1, Appendix B, Table 3) -- a directly analogous 'difficulty dial' concept to c23's rule-count/complexity dial, showing that a semantic/graph-distance dial is a viable alternative or complement to c23's rule-count dial.

Turn budget: capped at 20 turns per game; most successful games take 8-11 turns, with the best model (GPT-5.2-Chat) averaging 5.61 turns (Sec 3.1; Appendix E.1, Fig 8) -- relevant if c23 ever considers a bounded-interaction variant, though c23 itself is single-shot.

Metrics used: primary = binary Player Success Rate (exact rule match per game, oracle-judged); secondary = Confirmation bias (proportion of positive tests per game); Oracle accuracy/Cohen's kappa vs human annotation; conclusive falsification rate (per Sec 3.2, 4, 5) -- illustrates the value of a secondary process-diagnostic metric (like c23's 'how many of K rules stated' partial-credit idea) beyond simple pass/fail.

Floors/ceilings observed: best model 75% success (GPT-5.2-Chat), i.e., 'no model comes close to optimal performance' (Abstract, Sec 4) -- confirms that even a well-scoped rule-discovery-style task is far from saturated at the frontier, relevant to c23's own concern (flagged in c23.md as an 'Against' point) that InductionBench shows frontier models fail even the simplest complexity class, i.e. ceiling-reachability must be curated deliberately.

Prompt formats that mattered: strict JSON action schema enforced for both player and oracle roles, with a retry mechanism (up to 3 extra retries for malformed player JSON, 2 attempts for oracle JSON, with an added 'IMPORTANT: Return ONLY a JSON object' instruction on retry) (Appendix C.3) -- a directly reusable engineering pattern for any structured-output eval like c23's exact-match string transform.

Contamination/data-release status: code and data publicly released at https://github.com/leobertolazzi/FalsifyBench.git (footnote 2, header 'Code/Data'); authors deliberately avoided numeric Wason-2-4-6 items 'because LLMs are very likely to have encountered the classic 2-4-6 task during pretraining' (Sec 3.1) but used WordNet (a long-public, well-known lexical resource) for categories, so contamination is only partially mitigated -- contrasts with c23's stronger invented-vocabulary approach to contamination-proofing.

Cost data point: total experiment cost approx. $150 across 12 models x 100 games x 2 roles (Appendix D.1) -- useful order-of-magnitude reference for interactive-eval cost vs a single-shot design like c23.
quotes: "we introduce FalsifyBench, an evaluation framework for hypothesis-driven reasoning inspired by the classic Wason 2-4-6 task, in which agents must discover hidden semantic properties by iteratively proposing examples and receiving feedback." (Abstract)
"A game ended when either the player submitted a correct guess or the maximum turn limit was reached (we set the maximum number of turns to 20)." (Sec 3.1)

"The player was never shown the target rule R or the sampling category S directly; it observed only the three example items and the system prompt explaining the rules of the game (see Table 5)." (Appendix C.2)

"The best instruction-tuned model, DeepSeek-V3.1, reached only 41% success rate, while top reasoning models achieved 75% (GPT-5.2-Chat) and 68% (GPT-OSS-120B), with the remaining instruction-tuned models clustering between 33% and 35%." (Sec 4)

"we found a strong correlation between success rates and Confirmation bias: models that predominantly used positive testing consistently underperformed those that actively tested outside their current hypothesis (Spearman rho=-0.779, p=0.003)." (Sec 4)

"Confirmation bias was a strong negative predictor of success (posterior mean =-4.15, OR =0.016, posterior SD =0.116), while oracle error rate showed no credible independent effect (posterior mean =-0.12, OR =0.885, posterior SD =0.147), with a credible interval spanning zero." (Sec 4)

"Neither study explicitly takes into account the normative framework introduced by Klayman and Ha (1987): they neither sample triples from a rule that is a strict subset of the general target rule nor track how the hypotheses formed by models at each turn relate to the target rule." (Sec 2, re: WILT and Jhaveri et al. 2026)

"During the game interactions, the player was stateful: its conversation history accumulated all prior oracle feedback and its own previous actions. In contrast, the oracle was stateless: each call to the oracle was performed using a newly initialized prompt." (Appendix C.4)

corrections: GLM-5 was mislabeled 'instruction-tuned' in the models array. The source paper's own text (Sec 4: 'the remaining instruction-tuned models clustering between 33% and 35%' cannot include GLM-5's 58%; Sec 6: 'reasoning models showing particularly pronounced differences (GPT-5.2-Chat: +27.0pp, GPT-OSS-120B: +22.3pp, GLM-5: +21.4pp)' groups GLM-5 with reasoning models) shows GLM-5 is a reasoning model, not instruction-tuned. Fixed the models[].strategies label and key_results annotation for GLM-5.

failure_modes point (7) incorrectly stated that MiniMax-M2.5, GLM-5, and GPT-5-Nano all share the same oracle kappa range '0.67-0.91'. The source (Sec 4) gives three distinct ranges: MiniMax-M2.5 0.67-0.91, GLM-5 0.78/0.76, GPT-5-Nano 0.81/0.72. Fixed to list each model's actual range separately.

Llama-4-Maverick's 'nearly 8 guesses' figure was misattributed to the 'artifact' category specifically, in both the models[] entry and failure_modes point (4). The source (Appendix E.3) actually states this figure applies 'across all target properties' (i.e., uniformly high regardless of category), and a separate, unquantified claim about 'well-performing models' (not Llama-4-Maverick) showing elevated guessing specifically in the artifact category. Fixed both instances to remove the artifact-specific misattribution and quote the source's actual scope.
